title: Puppy Dragging Rear Legs: Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your puppy is dragging their back legs, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical for your puppy's mobility.
content_html:
What is it?

When a puppy drags their rear legs, it is a sign of hind limb paralysis or severe weakness, known medically as paraparesis. This occurs when the communication between the brain and the hind legs is disrupted, or when the physical structure of the spine or pelvis is compromised. It can manifest as a total loss of movement or a 'knuckling' gait where the puppy walks on the tops of their paws.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is an immediate medical emergency. A puppy dragging their back legs may be suffering from acute spinal cord trauma, a severe infection like canine distemper, or a congenital neurological defect. Because nerves are extremely sensitive and can die quickly without blood flow or proper treatment, any delay in veterinary care could lead to permanent, irreversible paralysis.

Why a Video Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so, capturing a 10-second video of your puppy attempting to move can be invaluable for the triage team. A video allows the veterinarian to see the specific nature of the movement, determine if there is any coordination remaining, and assess the severity of the neurological deficit before you even arrive at the clinic. This helps them prepare the necessary diagnostic equipment and prioritize your puppy for immediate evaluation.

faqs.question: Could my puppy just have a sore muscle?
faqs.answer: While muscle soreness can cause limping, it rarely results in a puppy dragging both rear legs. Dragging is typically a neurological or structural emergency rather than a simple strain.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my puppy to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the puppy as still as possible. Use a flat, firm surface like a piece of plywood or a sturdy crate to minimize movement of the spine during transport.

faqs.question: Is it okay to wait until the morning to see a vet?
faqs.answer: No. When it comes to neurological issues or spinal trauma, 'wait and see' is dangerous. Immediate intervention provides the best chance for a full recovery.
